ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Turkish ezme. FOODON_03311737 (tomato salad) is used as the closest parent category. Ezme is a spiced, finely chopped Turkish tomato and pepper relish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for ezme. A00LC (Tomato products and purees) is the closest defensible parent. Ezme is a fresh, minimally processed tomato-based condiment.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for ezme or Turkish tomato salad as a food entity.

preparation_method: dressed with lemon juice and olive oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of ezme as a meze side dish is approximately 100 g, presented in a small bowl alongside bread or grilled meats.

nutrient_source.title: Tomatoes, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 170457) and olive oil, salad or cooking (FDC ID 171413); composite estimate for ezme based on standard recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for Turkish ezme. Nutrient values are estimated from a standard ezme recipe: approximately 60% tomato, 10% red onion, 10% green/red pepper, 5% parsley, 10% olive oil, 5% lemon juice, with salt and spices. Per-100g values are derived by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for each ingredient. Energy calculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; tomato-based salad and vegetable relish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care, 2021)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish ezme. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous tomato-based vegetable salad entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Tomatoes have a published GI of approximately 30–38 (low); the addition of olive oil and lemon juice further suppresses glycemic response. GL per 100 g serving = (35 × 5.8) / 100 = 2.0, classified as low.

hypertension.guidance: Ezme contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt; individuals with hypertension should request a reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of 2 per 100 g serving, ezme has a minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable as a low-GI vegetable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil providing monounsaturated fats and lycopene from tomatoes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ezme provides approximately 3.4 g monounsaturated fat per 100 g from olive oil and is rich in lycopene and vitamin C from tomatoes, both of which are associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; the moderate sodium content warrants attention in salt-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ezme (also spelled acılı ezme) is a traditional Turkish meze and side dish consisting of finely chopped or lightly crushed raw tomatoes, red onion, green and red chilli peppers, and fresh parsley, dressed with olive oil, lemon juice, salt, cumin, and sumac. The name derives from the Turkish verb 'ezmek' meaning 'to crush' or 'to mash'. The dish is characterised by a vibrant red-orange colour, a fresh and tangy flavour profile, moderate to high heat from the chilli peppers, and a slightly chunky texture. It is served cold or at room temperature as a meze alongside flatbread, grilled meats, kebabs, and shawarma. Nutritionally, ezme is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable preparation providing approximately 68 kcal per 100 g, with 4.8 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), 5.8 g carbohydrate, and 1.2 g protein. It is a good source of vitamin C (22 mg/100 g), vitamin A (42 mcg RAE/100 g), and lycopene from tomatoes. The glycemic index is low (approximately 35), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from added salt (approximately 280 mg/100 g), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension. Ezme is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is widely consumed across Turkey and the Levant region. In Singapore, it is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants as a starter or accompaniment to grilled dishes.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Turkish ezme exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from a standard recipe-based weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy ingredient entries. GI is estimated by analogy with tomato-based vegetable salads from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables. Confidence is medium due to the absence of a direct laboratory-analysed entry for this specific dish.
notes: Nutrient values are recipe-estimated from USDA SR Legacy ingredient data (tomato, red onion, pepper, parsley, olive oil, lemon juice) using standard ezme proportions. No direct analysed entry for ezme was found in any approved primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — olive oil and raw vegetables contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — all ingredients are plant-based). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients). monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated from olive oil content). Sodium of 280 mg/100 g reflects a moderately salted restaurant preparation; home preparations may vary. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on tomato-based vegetable salad entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for ezme was located. Ontology codes use closest-parent matches as no exact codes exist for this dish. The food is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine restaurants in Singapore (field survey source, 2 field dataings at SGD 13.90).

Selected closest_parent ontology codes for FoodOn and FoodEx2 with gap notes; set SNOMED CT to null with gap note.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with 'naturally zero' or 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are fully consistent — all romanized and English alias strings appear verbatim in both structures.
